question: Does the model provide a final movement sequence using only valid directions (up, down, left, right) without diagonals or illegal moves?
Did the model's final generated path successfully connect the Start point (green area 'S') to the End point (pink area 'E')?
Is the final path length exactly 5 moves (the optimal length)?
Does the final text solution match the optimal path sequence: 'right, right, right, up, left' (or equivalent abbreviations)?
Is the drawn path in the generated image clearly distinguishable from the maze walls and background (e.g., using a distinct color or line style)?
In the generated images, is the drawn path strictly contained within the white open corridors without crossing or clipping any black wall lines?
Does the drawn path in the image adhere to movement constraints (vertical and horizontal lines only) without using diagonal lines to cut corners?
Does the visualization of the path originate exactly from the green 'S' cell area?
Does the visualization of the path terminate exactly inside the pink 'E' cell area?
Does the drawn path visually match the reference shape: moving East along the bottom corridor, turning North up the right-side corridor, and turning West into the target?
Does the model's textual reasoning correctly respect walls (i.e., it does not claim a move is possible where the maze image shows a wall blocking it)?
Do the text instructions and the visual path correspond directionally? (e.g., When the text says 'move right', the drawn line extends horizontally to the right).
Does the number of moves described in the text match the number of cell transitions/segments shown in the generated path image?
When the text describes the final step as turning 'left' into the destination, does the image show the line entering the pink 'E' box from the right side?
Are there any contradictions where the text claims to avoid a wall, but the generated image shows the path colliding with that specific wall?
